one_line_takeaway: By reframing LLM watermark sampling as a single constrained-optimization problem, the paper shows Red-Green/KGW, AAR/KTH, and SynthID are all instances of one framework differing only in their distortion constraint and score distribution, and uses this to derive new perplexity-constrained watermarks with state-of-the-art detectability-quality trade-offs while formally exposing an inherent quality-diversity-power trade-off.
key_finding: (1) Each watermark derived from a given constraint D is empirically Pareto-optimal with respect to that same constraint: Red-Green is Pareto-optimal for the KL constraint, the chi-squared scheme for the chi-squared constraint, and PPL-Soft for the soft-PPL constraint (Fig. 2), confirming the framework's derivations are not just formally correct but practically tight. (2) The newly derived PPL-Soft scheme achieves a state-of-the-art detectability-quality trade-off: at epsilon=0 (matched perplexity to the unwatermarked model) it outperforms AAR/KTH, which itself outperforms SynthID, and this ordering holds both under perplexity and under a separate LLM-benchmark-accuracy quality proxy (ruling out metric overfitting). (3) A genuine quality-diversity-power trade-off exists and was previously unquantified: at a fixed TPR@1=0.95, soft-constrained schemes (AAR/KTH, PPL-Soft) achieve the highest quality but the lowest diversity (highest self-BLEU), while hard-constrained schemes better preserve diversity at some quality cost; the paper proves (Theorem D.5) that soft-constraint-only watermarks are provably deterministic given a fixed prompt, which is the theoretical root cause of the diversity collapse.
core_contribution: The paper (Gloaguen, Staab, Jovanovic, Vechev, ETH Zurich) introduces a unified theoretical framework that casts the design of LLM watermarking sampling mechanisms as a constrained optimization problem: given a next-token distribution p and pseudorandom token scores g drawn from a distribution G (produced by a hashing mechanism), find a watermarked distribution q(g) that maximizes expected score (detectability) subject to a distortion constraint D(q(g), E_G[q(G)], p) <= epsilon. The authors show this single formulation recovers, as closed-form solutions under specific choices of the constraint D and the score distribution G, several previously 'unrelated' watermarking schemes from prior literature: Red-Green/KGW (hard KL constraint, Bernoulli G), AAR/Aaronson and Kuditipudi et al.'s EXP-edit/KTH (soft KL/distortion-free constraint, Gumbel G), and SynthID's 1-layer and m-layer tournament sampling (chi-squared constraint, Bernoulli G). Beyond recovering existing schemes, the framework is used generatively: the authors instantiate two genuinely new schemes -- a 'hard PPL' and a 'soft PPL' watermark that use per-token perplexity (relative to p) as the distortion constraint D, solved via linear programming (hard) or a Gumbel-based closed form with a root-found Lagrange multiplier (soft). The paper also derives and empirically confirms a 'quality-diversity-power' trade-off: watermarks built from hard constraints (bounding q(g) for every g) tend to preserve output diversity but sacrifice quality/power, whereas soft-constraint watermarks (bounding only E_G[q(G)]) are proven (Theorem D.5) to be deterministic given a fixed prompt, hence maximize quality and detection power but collapse output diversity.
threat_model: Not an attacker/defender security paper in the adversarial-robustness sense; the paper's own framing is provenance/detection, not attack. The stated goal of LLM watermarking (per Sec. 2, restating prior work by Kirchenbauer et al. 2023) is to let a model-free, computationally efficient detector determine post hoc whether a given text was sampled from a watermarked LLM, by testing whether pseudorandom per-token scores (derived from a hash of prior context) are anomalously high. The paper explicitly does not model paraphrasing, spoofing, or scrubbing attacks against the hashing mechanism or detector -- it states in Related Work that such robustness/anti-spoofing work (Hou et al. Semstamp, Zhou et al. Bileve, Shen et al.) is orthogonal and cites it only as context, not as something this paper improves. The Impact Statement notes watermarks 'support provenance, auditing, and mitigation of large-scale AI-generated text misuse' but explicitly cautions they 'should not be used as sole evidence in major decisions' -- this is the paper's own caveat, not the reviewer's addition.
limitations: The authors state their own limitations explicitly: (1) the optimization is restricted to the token level rather than the sequence level; (2) they optimize only the sampling mechanism and deliberately do not jointly optimize the detector, even though jointly-optimized approaches (e.g., He et al. 2024) might achieve better overall performance; (3) the framework does not capture all watermarking algorithms in the literature -- notably fully cryptographic watermarks are explicitly excluded. Additionally (noted within the paper's ablation, not as a caveat but as a finding) the framework gives no guidance on which score distribution G to choose to maximize power for a given constraint -- this must be found empirically and differs per scheme (e.g., soft-PPL degrades with heavier-tailed G, the opposite of a finding reported by Tsur et al. 2025a for AAR-style schemes), and the chi-squared watermark under a Bernoulli G exhibits catastrophic failure beyond a certain distortion threshold.

datasets_used: ELI5 (Fan et al., 2019) — 1,000 prompts for main detectability/quality trade-off experiments, 200-token generations
ELI5 subset of 100 prompts x 100 samples/prompt (10,000 total generations) for the self-BLEU diversity protocol
quantitative_results: Detectability measured as TPR at 1% FPR across all schemes (Fig. 2, 3, 5, 6, 7)
At epsilon=0 (matched perplexity), ordering by detectability-quality trade-off: PPL (Soft) > AAR/KTH > SynthID

Diversity measured via trigram self-BLEU (0-1 scale) at fixed TPR@1=0.95; soft-constraint schemes (AAR/KTH, PPL-Soft) show highest self-BLEU (lowest diversity) but highest quality; hard-constraint schemes show the opposite
SynthID configured with a 30-layer tournament using Bernoulli(0.5) g-values in the experiments
Monte Carlo approximation with 128 samples used to estimate E_G[q(G)] for soft-constraint schemes
Score-distribution ablation covers 6 distributions: Bernoulli(0.5), Binomial(30,0.5), Gumbel(0,1), lognormal(0,1), normal(0,1), uniform[0,1]
Replication performed on a second model, MINISTRAL-3-14B, in addition to the primary LLAMA3.1-8B experiments
